content: {"answer": "The search results document the general regulatory frameworks for health claims at FDA (Sources 1-4, 6-8, 10) and EFSA (Sources 11-15), but contain limited IF-specific substantiation analysis. FDA's Authorized Health Claims require 'significant scientific agreement' (SSA) among qualified experts, a high bar codified in 21 CFR Part 101 Subpart E (Sources 1, 3, 10). A lower-tier Qualified Health Claim path exists where evidence is 'supported by scientific evidence but does not meet the more rigorous SSA standard' \u2014 claims are permitted with required disclaimers (Sources 2, 6, 7). EFSA's framework (Regulation EC 1924/2006) likewise distinguishes functional claims from disease-risk-reduction claims; the latter requires demonstrated beneficial alteration of a risk factor in the development of a disease (Sources 11-15). Source 5 offers one IF-specific observation: 'Scientific evidence indicates that periodic energy restriction leads to improved insulin sensitivity, lower blood pressure, and reduced risk of' \u2014 but the fetched excerpt is truncated and not sufficient to assess whether the underlying evidence meets SSA or QHC thresholds. Source 9 (CNN lay coverage) reports the state of IF science is contested: 'One day intermittent fasting is good for your health, the next it's not.' The provided sources do NOT directly enumerate which specific adverse events (appendicular lean mass loss, hypoglycemia in insulin users, eating-disorder precipitation) would trigger a consumer advisory. No Health Canada documents are present in the search results. The results are therefore adequate to describe the GENERAL regulatory decision architecture but NOT adequate to determine whether current IF evidence meets SSA/QHC/EFSA thresholds or which specific adverse events would cross each jurisdiction's advisory threshold. Information missing: IF-specific SSA assessments, any qualified-health-claim petitions for IF, EFSA NDA Panel opinions on fasting, Health Canada dietary guidance on IF.", "key_findings": ["FDA SSA standard: 'significant scientific agreement among qualified experts' codified in 21 CFR Part 101 Subpart E (Sources 1, 3, 10).", "FDA Qualified Health Claim tier available with disclaimers for evidence below SSA (Sources 2, 6, 7).", "EFSA disease-risk-reduction claims governed by Regulation EC 1924/2006; require beneficial alteration of a risk factor for disease development (Sources 11, 13, 14).", "No IF-specific SSA, QHC, or EFSA opinion document surfaced in the search results.", "Lay coverage (Source 9) characterizes IF evidence as contested; Source 5 describes general periodic-energy-restriction benefits but is truncated."], "sources_used": ["https://www.fda.gov/food/nutrition-food-labeling-and-critical-foods/authorized-health-claims-meet-significant-scientific-agreement-ssa-standard", "https://www.fda.gov/food/nutrition-food-labeling-and-critical-foods/qualified-health-claims", "https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-21/chapter-I/subchapter-B/part-101/subpart-E", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12740946/", "https://www.efsa.europa.eu/sites/default/files/event/101202/nda101202-p02.pdf", "https://www.efsa.europa.eu/en/topics/topic/health-claims", "https://edition.cnn.com/2024/03/19/health/intermittent-fasting-pros-cons-wellness"]}
